htmlContent:
Why the calendar feels like a minefield

I used to dread November. Not for the weather or the shorter days, but for what was coming. The holiday season. A stretch of weeks that felt less like celebration and more like an endurance test. You know the feeling, right? That knot in your stomach when someone asks what you are doing for Thanksgiving. The way your chest tightens when you see the first string of lights go up in the neighborhood.

Here's the thing. For trauma survivors, holidays are not just stressful. They are neurologically activating in ways that most people cannot understand. Your nervous system does not see twinkling lights and family gatherings. It sees unpredictability. It sees obligation. It sees the same relational patterns that originally taught your body to stay on high alert.

I have spent years studying how trauma reshapes the nervous system. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, would tell you that holidays throw your autonomic nervous system into chaos. Your vagus nerve, the main highway between your brain and your body, is constantly scanning for safety or danger. During holidays, that scan goes into overdrive. Family dynamics. Crowded rooms. The pressure to perform happiness. Your body does not know the difference between a loud uncle and a genuine threat. It just knows something feels wrong.

And it is not just the big stuff. It is the small, cumulative hits. The smell of a certain candle. The sound of a specific song. The way someone clears their throat the same way your abuser did. Your brain catalogs all of it. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, called this "implicit memory" - the body remembering what the mind has tried to forget. Holidays are a sensory minefield because they flood you with cues that your system has learned to associate with danger.

I remember one Christmas where I spent the entire dinner counting the ceiling tiles. Not because I was bored. Because my body had gone completely numb. That is dissociation, the brain's last-resort survival strategy when fleeing or fighting are not options. And it happens constantly during holidays because the pressure to stay present and "normal" is so intense that the only way to cope is to leave your own body.

So if you have ever felt like the only person in the room who is barely holding it together, you are not alone. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it was trained to do. It is trying to protect you from harm that, in the past, was very real.

The performance of joy and the cost of pretending

There is this unspoken rule during holidays: you must be happy. You must be grateful. You must participate in the collective performance of joy. And if you cannot, something is wrong with you.

That is a lie. A dangerous one.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He wrote about how survivors often develop a "false self" - a version of themselves that complies with social expectations while their true experience remains hidden. Holidays demand this false self more than any other time of year. You smile when you want to scream. You say "I'm fine" when you are drowning. You hug people who have hurt you because that is what families do.

I have done it. I bet you have too. And every time you do, you reinforce the message that your real feelings are unacceptable. That your body's honest reactions are something to be ashamed of. That connection requires abandoning yourself.

Stay with me here. This is not about blaming yourself for surviving. It is about seeing the trap clearly. The wellness industry loves to sell you on "choosing joy" and "ing abundance" during the holidays. But that advice lands on trauma survivors like salt in an open wound. It tells you that your pain is a failure of attitude rather than a natural response to what you have endured. Toxic positivity is not healing. It is gaslighting with a bow on top.

I have stopped pretending. Not completely, because healing is not linear and I still slip into old patterns. But I have started telling myself the truth: I do not owe anyone a performance of happiness. My body's discomfort is valid data. And if I need to leave a gathering early, or skip it entirely, that is not a moral failure. It is self-preservation.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker. He writes specifically about the emotional flashbacks that holidays trigger - those sudden waves of feeling small, abandoned, or terrified that seem to come from nowhere. Walker calls them "emotional flashbacks" and explains how they are not memories of events but memories of feelings. Your body relives the helplessness, the shame, the loneliness, even if your mind cannot connect them to a specific incident. That book helped me understand why I could be at a beautifully decorated table with people who supposedly loved me and still feel like a terrified child.

The grief that no one talks about

Holidays are also a time of grief for trauma survivors. And I do not just mean grief over lost loved ones. I mean grief over the childhood you never had. The family you needed but did not get. The safety that was stolen from you before you had words for it.

I see Christmas commercials with laughing children and warm kitchens, and something in my chest cracks open. Not because I am jealous. Because I am mourning. I am mourning the version of myself who deserved that and never got it. The version who learned too early that love was conditional, that safety was a transaction, that the people who were supposed to protect you could also be the ones who hurt you.

Gabor Mate, a physician who has written extensively about trauma and addiction, often says that trauma is not what happens to you but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. The holidays trigger that internal wound repeatedly. The contrast between what is "supposed" to be and what actually was can be unbearable.

I have learned to make space for that grief. To let myself feel it without trying to fix it or explain it away. Some nights I sit on my couch and just let the sadness wash through me. It does not feel good. But it feels true. And truth, I have found, is more healing than forced positivity ever was.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty by Jonice Webb. She describes childhood emotional neglect as the invisible trauma - not what happened, but what did not happen. The attunement that was missing. The comfort that was withheld. The validation that never came. Holidays that absence. You are surrounded by images of connection while carrying the weight of a lifetime of disconnection. Webb's work helped me name that emptiness and start filling it with something real.

Practical ways to survive the season

I am not going to give you a checklist of "10 Easy Steps to Holiday Bliss." That is not how this works. But I can share what has actually helped me and the survivors I talk to. These are not cures. They are tools. And tools only work if you use them, so take what fits and leave the rest.

First, give yourself permission to opt out. You do not have to attend every gathering you are invited to. You do not have to explain yourself. A simple "I cannot make it this year" is a complete sentence. Your nervous system does not need a justification. It needs safety.

Second, create exit strategies. If you do go to an event, have a plan for leaving. Drive yourself. Park where you can leave easily. Set a time limit. I have a friend who texts me before family dinners and I text her when I am safely home. That small accountability makes a huge difference.

Third, regulate your nervous system before and after. This is where tools come in. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is the Breathing Exercise Tool that extends your exhale longer than your inhale. Extended exhales activate the vagus nerve and signal safety to your brain. I use mine before walking into any high-stress situation and again when I get home. It is not magic. But it shifts something in the body that makes the rest possible.

Fourth, stay grounded in your own story. If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn. He explores how trauma can be passed down through generations, showing up in your body and your reactions even if you never experienced the original event. That perspective helped me stop blaming myself for feelings that were never mine to begin with.

Fifth, let yourself feel whatever comes. If you need to cry, cry. If you need to rage, find a safe way to let that out. If you need to be alone, be alone. Your feelings are not the enemy. They are your nervous system trying to communicate with you. Listen to them. Honor them. Do not push them away because the calendar says you should be merry.

The truth about healing

I wish I could tell you that the holidays get easier. That with enough therapy and enough tools, the dread disappears. But that has not been my experience. What has changed is my relationship to the difficulty. I no longer fight it. I no longer shame myself for it. I accept that this time of year is hard for me, and that is okay.

Healing from trauma is not about becoming someone who is never triggered. It is about becoming someone who can be triggered and still stay connected to themselves. Who can feel the old pain without being consumed by it. Who can sit in the discomfort and know, deep in their bones, that they are going to survive.

Sit with that. Your survival is not a flaw. It is your strength. And if this holiday season is hard, if you are struggling to keep your head above water, I see you. I am right there with you. And we do not have to pretend otherwise.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving - Pete Walker's guide to understanding and healing from complex trauma
  • Running on Empty - Jonice Webb's exploration of childhood emotional neglect
  • Breathing Exercise Tool - A device designed to extend the exhale for nervous system regulation
  • It Didn't Start with You - Mark Wolynn's research on inherited family trauma
